audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Sarai/Hagar/Abram household power, slave-woman language, affliction, angel/messenger of the Eternal, Ishmael naming, El Roi, and Beer-lahai-roi. Final source-critical review may refine legal/social context.

verses.restored: Sarai, Abram’s woman, had borne him no child. She had an Egyptian slave-woman, and her name was Hagar.

familiar.text: Now Sarai Abram's wife bare him no children: and she had an handmaid, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar.

notes.title: Slave-woman
notes.body: Shifchah means female servant/slave. Handmaid softens the power imbalance.

verses.restored: Sarai said to Abram, Look, the Eternal has restrained me from bearing. Please go in to my slave-woman; perhaps I will be built up through her. Abram listened to the voice of Sarai.

familiar.text: And Sarai said unto Abram, Behold now, the LORD hath restrained me from bearing...

notes.title: Built up through her
notes.body: Sarai seeks household continuity through Hagar’s body. The phrase exposes the social logic but does not make it morally simple.

notes.title: Listened to the voice
notes.body: The phrase echoes earlier Genesis scenes and signals a pivotal choice.

verses.restored: Sarai, Abram’s woman, took Hagar the Egyptian, her slave-woman, after Abram had dwelled ten years in the land of Canaan, and gave her to Abram her man as a woman.

familiar.text: And Sarai Abram's wife took Hagar her maid the Egyptian... and gave her to her husband Abram to be his wife.

notes.title: Took and gave
notes.body: The verbs took and gave emphasize Sarai’s control in the arrangement and Hagar’s limited agency.

verses.restored: He went in to Hagar, and she conceived. When she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was light in her eyes.

familiar.text: And he went in unto Hagar, and she conceived: and when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was despised in her eyes.

notes.title: Light in her eyes
notes.body: Qalal means to be light, slight, or dishonored. Despised is possible, but the eye-language should be preserved.

verses.restored: Sarai said to Abram, My wrong be upon you. I gave my slave-woman into your embrace, and when she saw that she had conceived, I was light in her eyes. May the Eternal judge between me and you.

familiar.text: And Sarai said unto Abram, My wrong be upon thee... the LORD judge between me and thee.

notes.title: My wrong be upon you
notes.body: Sarai names harm or wrong, but directs blame toward Abram after a plan involving all three.

verses.restored: Abram said to Sarai, Look, your slave-woman is in your hand. Do to her what is good in your eyes. Then Sarai afflicted her, and she fled from her face.

familiar.text: But Abram said unto Sarai, Behold, thy maid is in thy hand... And when Sarai dealt hardly with her, she fled...

notes.title: Afflicted her
notes.body: Anah means afflict, oppress, or humble. The same root later describes Israel’s affliction in Egypt.

notes.title: In your hand
notes.body: Abram returns control over Hagar to Sarai rather than protecting her.

verses.restored: The messenger of the Eternal found her by a spring of water in the wilderness, by the spring on the way to Shur.

familiar.text: And the angel of the LORD found her by a fountain of water in the wilderness...

notes.title: Messenger of the Eternal
notes.body: Malakh YHWH can be rendered angel or messenger of the Eternal. Messenger keeps the functional meaning visible.

verses.restored: He said, Hagar, slave-woman of Sarai, where have you come from, and where are you going? She said, I am fleeing from the face of Sarai my mistress.

familiar.text: And he said, Hagar, Sarai's maid, whence camest thou? and whither wilt thou go?...

notes.title: Named in the wilderness
notes.body: The messenger addresses Hagar by name, one of the few direct divine encounters with an enslaved foreign woman in Genesis.

verses.restored: The messenger of the Eternal said to her, Return to your mistress and humble yourself under her hands.

familiar.text: And the angel of the LORD said unto her, Return to thy mistress, and submit thyself under her hands.

notes.title: Difficult command
notes.body: The command to return and humble herself is hard. It should not be generalized as approval of abuse; it belongs to this ancient survival story with a promise attached.

verses.restored: The messenger of the Eternal said to her, I will greatly multiply your seed, so that it cannot be counted for multitude.

familiar.text: And the angel of the LORD said unto her, I will multiply thy seed exceedingly...

notes.title: Seed promise to Hagar
notes.body: Hagar receives a seed-multiplication promise directly, echoing Abram’s promise.

verses.restored: The messenger of the Eternal said to her, Look, you are pregnant and shall bear a son. You shall call his name Ishmael, for the Eternal has heard your affliction.

familiar.text: And the angel of the LORD said unto her, Behold, thou art with child... call his name Ishmael...

notes.title: Ishmael
notes.body: Ishmael means God hears. The name is tied to the Eternal hearing Hagar’s affliction.

verses.restored: He shall be a wild donkey of a human; his hand against everyone, and everyone’s hand against him. He shall dwell before the face of all his brothers.

familiar.text: And he will be a wild man; his hand will be against every man...

notes.title: Wild donkey of a human
notes.body: Pere adam is literally wild donkey of a human. The image suggests untamed freedom and conflict, not a simple racial insult.

notes.title: Before the face of his brothers
notes.body: The phrase can mean in the presence of, alongside, or facing his brothers.

verses.restored: She called the name of the Eternal who spoke to her, You are El Roi, God of seeing, for she said, Have I even here looked after the One who sees me?

familiar.text: And she called the name of the LORD that spake unto her, Thou God seest me...

notes.title: El Roi
notes.body: Hagar names God through being seen. This is a profound theological moment from an enslaved Egyptian woman.

notes.title: Difficult Hebrew
notes.body: The second half of the verse is difficult. The rendering preserves the seeing/being-seen wordplay.

verses.restored: Therefore the well was called Beer-lahai-roi. Look, it is between Kadesh and Bered.

familiar.text: Wherefore the well was called Beerlahairoi; behold, it is between Kadesh and Bered.

notes.title: Beer-lahai-roi
notes.body: The name is commonly understood as well of the Living One who sees me, preserving Hagar’s encounter in place-memory.

verses.restored: Hagar bore Abram a son, and Abram called the name of his son, whom Hagar bore, Ishmael.

familiar.text: And Hagar bare Abram a son: and Abram called his son's name, which Hagar bare, Ishmael.

verses.restored: Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael to Abram.

familiar.text: And Abram was fourscore and six years old, when Hagar bare Ishmael to Abram.
